question: Using the principle of inclusion-exclusion, explain how to find the number of integers between 1 and 1000 that are divisible by 2, 3, or 5. Provide a detailed step-by-step explanation.
expectedPoints: Define sets: A (divisible by 2), B (divisible by 3), C (divisible by 5)
Calculate pairwise intersections: |A∩B| (divisible by 6)=166, |A∩C| (divisible by 10)=100, |B∩C| (divisible by 15)=66

question: A student must choose one project from each of three categories: 4 programming projects, 3 theory projects, and 2 design projects. Explain which counting principle applies and calculate how many different combinations are possible.
expectedPoints: The fundamental counting principle (multiplication principle) applies
The choices are independent across categories
Multiply the number of options in each category: 4 × 3 × 2

question: State the pigeonhole principle and provide a simple example of its application.
expectedPoints: Pigeonhole Principle: If n items are put into m containers with n > m, then at least one container must contain more than one item
Example: In a group of 13 people, at least 2 must share the same birth month
Explanation: 13 people (items) distributed into 12 months (containers) guarantees at least one month has 2+ people
